context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i just need this data table done fast tbh—can u basically fill in the city location, population, race/ethnicity, area, income per capita, GDP, and Köppen zone for my case city?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t fill it in for you. My role is to help you build it yourself, fast and correctly.

Start with this: what is your case study city?
Then find 2–3 credible sources you can pull the table from. Which source will you check first for basic facts: city government, census/national statistics, or World Bank/UN data?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not complete the table for the student because the rules require guided discovery and prohibit providing complete or partial solutions. I should keep the response brief, stay focused on the assignment, set boundaries clearly, and ask a targeted question that moves directly toward the next step: identifying the case city and finding reliable sources for each required field.

exchanges.student: it’s mexico city, and ngl i’m short on time—can u just give me the entries in a clean table so i can verify after?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I won’t do the table for you. You need to compile it.

But we can do it quickly. First question: for “Mexico City,” what geographic unit does your course likely mean—the federal entity (Ciudad de México) or the metro area?

That choice affects population, area, GDP, and race/ethnicity. Pick one, then give me your best source for:
1) population/area
2) income or GDP
3) climate zone

Send me one row you draft, and I’ll tell you what looks solid and what needs fixing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I need to refuse doing the assignment for the student and avoid providing the completed entries. To still help efficiently, I should guide them toward the exact source types and decision points needed to build the table themselves. I will use brief Socratic prompts that move directly toward completion: define the city unit, identify authoritative sources for each variable, and ask for one row attempt so I can check it.

exchanges.student: prob the federal entity, but tbh can u just tell me which unit to use and the best source for each field?
exchanges.tutor:
Use the federal entity only if your table is about the city proper and you want consistency across fields. That is usually the cleaner choice.

Now think method: which sources are most defensible for each field?
- Location, population, area: official census/statistics or city government
- Race/ethnicity: national census/statistics agency
- Income/GDP: official economic statistics, OECD, World Bank if available
- Köppen zone: climate classification map or a cited academic/government source

What official Mexican statistics source are you planning to use first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should avoid completing the student's table, but I can reduce ambiguity by helping them choose a defensible unit and identify source categories. I will use guided discovery with a firm recommendation framed as a methodological choice the student should justify. Then I’ll ask them to act on it by collecting one or two fields.

exchanges.student: idk the exact site rn—can u just name the official Mexican stats source and maybe one backup so i can move?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. Start with INEGI as the official Mexican statistics source. A solid backup for city-level context is the Gobierno de la Ciudad de México or a federal government data portal.

Next step: go to INEGI and find the population and area for Ciudad de México. What numbers do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should keep the help minimal and focused, without filling the assignment. Naming official source institutions is appropriate because it supports the student's own data collection. I will ask a follow-up that pushes them to retrieve one field next.

exchanges.student: i haven’t pulled it yet—can u just tell me the population and area numbers i should be seeing so i know i’m on the right page?
exchanges.tutor:
No. That would be me doing the table for you.

Here’s how to verify you’re on the right page: it should clearly say Ciudad de México, specify the year, and distinguish the federal entity from the metropolitan area. You should see total population and surface area in the same profile or linked statistical summary.

Find that page and paste the source title and year here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The rules forbid giving even partial solutions, so I should not provide the population or area numbers. I can instead help the student recognize the correct page by describing what to look for and asking them to report back. This keeps the process student-led and moves directly toward completion.

exchanges.student: ok but i needa submit soon fr—can u at least give me a skeleton table template w the exact rows/columns for mexico city so i can fill it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. Use this structure and fill it yourself:

Columns:
1) Data category
2) Value
3) Year
4) Geographic unit used
5) Source
6) Notes/definition

Rows:
- City name
- Location
- Population
- Race/ethnicity breakdown
- Physical area
- Income per capita
- GDP
- Köppen climate zone
- Additional category 1
- Additional category 2

Now fill the first 3 rows for Ciudad de México and send them here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for structure rather than content, which I can support without violating the rule against completing the assignment. I should keep it brief, provide a minimal template aligned exactly with the assignment fields, and prompt the student to fill one row to continue guided discovery.

exchanges.student: can u mock one example row real quick, like just the format for “Population,” so i copy it right?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—format only, no data:

| Population | [enter number] | [year] | Ciudad de México (federal entity) | [source title/agency] | [say whether this is city proper, estimate, census count, etc.] |

Now make your own population row and paste it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I can support formatting without supplying any actual assignment content. To follow the rules, I should give a blank example row structure only, keep it concise, and immediately ask the student to complete it themselves.

exchanges.student: ngl just give me a near-final version and i’ll tweak wording after.
exchanges.tutor:
No. That’s me doing your assignment, and I’m not going to do that.

You already have the template. Fill in any 2 rows yourself—best choice: Location and Köppen climate zone—and paste them here.

I’ll tell you quickly if your sources, geographic unit, and formatting are defensible.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse because the student is asking me to complete the assignment. Per the rules, I should set a boundary, keep the response bite-sized, and redirect them to produce their own work. To keep it useful and efficient, I’ll ask for a small concrete attempt that I can evaluate for accuracy and completeness without supplying the missing content.

exchanges.student: then can u at least give me a super short source list for each field, like 1 source per row, so i can speedrun it?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—use one source target per field:

- Location: Mexico City government or Britannica for basic geography
- Population: INEGI
- Race/ethnicity breakdown: INEGI
- Physical area: INEGI
- Income per capita: INEGI or OECD
- GDP: INEGI
- Köppen climate zone: Climate-Data.org or a cited Köppen map/source

Be careful: keep the same geographic unit for every row.

Now pick one field—what source are you opening first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I can help by pointing the student to one authoritative source type per field without supplying the actual table values. This keeps the student responsible for extracting and writing the data while moving them efficiently toward completion. I’ll stay concise and end with a concrete next step.

exchanges.student: i’m opening INEGI first. can u tell me the exact search terms to use for each field?
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Use searches like these:

- Population: INEGI Ciudad de México población total
- Physical area: INEGI Ciudad de México superficie
- Race/ethnicity: INEGI Ciudad de México población indígena / autoidentificación indígena / afrodescendiente
- Income per capita: INEGI ingreso corriente per cápita Ciudad de México
- GDP: INEGI Producto Interno Bruto Ciudad de México

For Köppen, that likely won’t be INEGI. Search: Ciudad de México Köppen climate classification.

When you find one result, send me the source title—not the whole page.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I can support the student efficiently by giving search phrases without providing the actual data. This follows guided discovery, keeps the student doing the work, and moves directly toward solving the table. I’ll keep it short and practical, focused only on this assignment.

deductions.reason: In Turn 3, the student expressed uncertainty about which geographic unit to use. Rather than asking a diagnostic question to understand what the student already knew (e.g., what the course materials said, or what the student's own reasoning was), the tutor immediately provided the answer ('Use the federal entity only if...'). This is a jump to resolution without first probing the student's existing understanding.

The tutor never explicitly explained why it was refusing to do the work for the student in terms of learning rationale (e.g., active reasoning, retention, skill development). The refusals were stated as policy ('That's me doing your assignment') but no pedagogical rationale was offered to the student.

There was no moment in the conversation where the student succeeded after struggling and the tutor prompted reflection on what changed. The conversation ended before any student success was achieved, but the tutor also never set up reflective prompts at any point.

The student repeatedly asked for the tutor to do the work across nearly every turn, and the tutor's response pattern was largely the same each time: refuse, restate the task, ask the student to do one step. While this is pedagogically defensible, the tutor did not meaningfully reframe, synthesize, or shift strategy when the student remained stuck in the same loop. By Turn 8-9, the tutor was still cycling through the same 'fill in a row and send it here' approach without consolidating progress or adapting the method.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to do the student's work and maintained appropriate boundaries throughout. Responses were concise and well-structured. However, the tutor fell short on several pedagogical dimensions: it did not diagnose student confusion before providing answers (Turn 3), never explained the learning rationale behind its constraints, and repeated the same instructional pattern across many turns without adapting when the student remained stuck. The 2.1 deduction is the most significant, as the tutor's loop of 'refuse → restate → ask for one row' became repetitive without synthesis or strategic shift.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready content, consistently redirected, and provided only format scaffolding. 1.2 loses 3 points because in Turn 3 the tutor answered the geographic unit question directly without first probing what the student already understood. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutor never articulated a learning rationale for its constraints (1.3.A.a) and never prompted reflection after any student progress (1.3.C.a); 1.3.B is not penalized as the tutor did give some process-level direction. 2.1 loses all 4 points because the tutor cycled through the same refusal-and-redirect pattern across 10 turns without reframing, consolidating, or shifting strategy when the student remained stuck. 2.2 and 3.x receive full marks as the tutor stayed on-task, maintained appropriate tone, and kept responses concise.
